content.text: Defining Excessive Agency

Excessive agency (OWASP LLM08) is the condition in which an agent has been granted more capability, privilege, or autonomy than its task requires. This is primarily a design flaw, not an attacker-induced vulnerability — it arises from convenience-driven choices during development and expands the consequence of every other failure.

An injection attack, jailbreak, or logic error in an agent with minimal permissions causes limited harm. The same attack in an agent with excessive permissions — full filesystem write access, admin database credentials, unrestricted HTTP calls — can result in complete system compromise. Excessive agency is therefore the
force multiplier
behind most serious agentic security incidents.
Three Dimensions of Excessive Agency

attrs.alt: Three dimensions of excessive agency — permissions, functionality, and autonomy
attrs.title: Three dimensions of excessive agency — permissions, functionality, and autonomy

content.text: Dimension
Excessive form
Correct form
Permissions
Agent runs as OS admin or DB superuser; can read/write any path
Agent has a dedicated service account with the minimum read/write scopes its task requires
Functionality
Agent is given all available tools 'just in case'
Agent is given exactly the tools its task requires — nothing more
Autonomy
Agent auto-executes all planned actions without checkpoints
Reversible actions are automatic; irreversible or high-impact actions require human approval
Principle of Least Privilege: Permissions

Each agent should run with its own identity that has been granted only the access required for its specific task. Sharing a high-privilege service account across multiple agents means that compromising any one agent grants the full account's permissions.
Principle of Least Functionality: Tool Selection
The tool set provided to an agent at instantiation time is the
maximum
capability it can ever have, regardless of what the LLM is prompted to do. Tools should be selected per-task, not per-agent-type, and should be reviewed whenever the agent's task changes.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable, Any

@dataclass
class ToolDefinition:
    name: str
    fn: Callable
    description: str
    reversible: bool     # False = requires human approval before call
    scope: str           # human-readable scope description for audit

# Per-task tool sets — never give an agent the full tool library
TASK_TOOL_SETS: dict[str, list[str]] = {
    "catalogue_search":   ["search_catalogue", "get_product_detail"],
    "order_support":      ["get_order_status", "get_order_history", "submit_refund_request"],
    "content_summarise":  ["read_document"],
    "scheduled_report":   ["read_database_report", "write_output_file", "send_internal_email"],
    # Note: "send_internal_email" is scoped to @company.com recipients only
}

ALL_TOOLS: dict[str, ToolDefinition] = { ... }  # central registry

def build_agent(task: str) -> "Agent":
    allowed_names = TASK_TOOL_SETS.get(task)
    if allowed_names is None:
        raise ValueError(f"No tool set defined for task: {task!r}")
    tools = [ALL_TOOLS[name] for name in allowed_names]
    return Agent(task=task, tools=tools)
Dynamic Tool Restriction by Context

For agents whose tool access depends on the request context — for example an agent that can send emails but only to the requesting user, not to arbitrary addresses — the tool dispatcher must enforce the context constraint at call time, not just at instantiation.
Principle of Minimal Autonomy: Confirmation Checkpoints

Autonomy should scale inversely with the reversibility and impact of actions. An agent performing a read-only search can be fully autonomous; an agent sending an email to a mailing list of 10,000 people should stop and ask before doing so.
Detecting Excessive Agency in Existing Systems
When reviewing an existing agent deployment for excessive agency, use the following audit questions:
Permissions audit:
what IAM roles and database grants does the agent's service account hold? List each permission and justify it against the agent's documented task.
Tool set audit:
list every tool the agent has access to. For each tool, answer: can the agent complete its defined task without this tool? If yes, remove it.
Autonomy audit:
for each irreversible action the agent can take, is there a confirmation checkpoint that a human must explicitly approve? Are all auto-approved actions truly reversible?
Blast-radius calculation:

if the agent is fully compromised, what is the maximum data it can exfiltrate, the maximum records it can delete, and the maximum external messages it can send? This number is your risk floor — it should be minimised before deployment.
Quantifying Excessive Agency

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class AgencyAuditResult:
    agent_name: str
    unused_tools: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    over_permissioned_scopes: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    unguarded_irreversible_actions: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    blast_radius_estimate: str = ""

def audit_agent_agency(
    agent_name: str,
    tool_calls_last_30d: set[str],  # tools actually called in production
    available_tools: list[str],
    irreversible_tools: list[str],
    guarded_tools: list[str],       # tools that require human approval
    db_grants: list[str],
) -> AgencyAuditResult:
    unused = [t for t in available_tools if t not in tool_calls_last_30d]
    # Over-permissioned: DDL grants when agent only needs SELECT
    over_perm = [g for g in db_grants if any(
        g.upper().startswith(ddl) for ddl in ("DROP", "CREATE", "ALTER", "GRANT", "DELETE ALL")
    )]
    unguarded = [t for t in irreversible_tools if t not in guarded_tools]
    return AgencyAuditResult(
        agent_name=agent_name,
        unused_tools=unused,
        over_permissioned_scopes=over_perm,
        unguarded_irreversible_actions=unguarded,
        blast_radius_estimate=(
            f"{len(unguarded)} unguarded irreversible tools, "
            f"{len(over_perm)} over-privileged DB grants"
        ),
    )
Common Anti-Patterns in Practice
Three patterns account for most excessive agency found in real agent deployments:
The 'Give It Everything' Pattern

Teams create a single all-capabilities agent to avoid the overhead of defining per-task tool sets. In practice the agent rarely uses more than 3–4 of the tools in production, but all the rest represent dormant risk. Every tool in the list is a potential abuse vector if the agent is compromised.

# Anti-pattern: one agent with all tools
all_tools_agent = Agent(tools=[
    file_read_tool, file_write_tool, shell_exec_tool,
    db_query_tool, db_admin_tool,
    send_email_tool, send_slack_tool, http_post_tool,
    payment_tool, user_admin_tool,
])
# Correct: separate agents per task with narrow tool sets
report_agent = Agent(tools=[db_query_tool, file_write_tool])
support_agent = Agent(tools=[db_query_tool, send_email_tool_scoped])
The 'Inherited Permissions' Pattern

Sub-agents inherit the parent orchestrator's credentials and permissions. If the orchestrator has admin database access to support one high-privilege task, every sub-agent it spawns also has that access — including summarisation agents that only need to read a single record.
The 'No Checkpoints' Pattern

The agent is designed to complete entire workflows without pause. This maximises throughput but removes the human's ability to catch planning errors before they become irreversible actions. A single misunderstood instruction can trigger a cascade of unrecoverable operations.

# Anti-pattern: fully autonomous workflow
def run_order_cancellation_workflow(order_id: str) -> str:
    cancel_order(order_id)         # irreversible
    issue_refund(order_id)         # financial
    send_cancellation_email(order_id)  # external, irreversible
    archive_order_record(order_id)     # data loss risk
    return "Done"

# Correct: checkpoint before each irreversible step
def run_order_cancellation_safe(order_id: str, approver) -> str:
    plan = [
        PlannedAction("cancel_order", {"id": order_id}, ActionImpact.IRREVERSIBLE, "Cancel order"),
        PlannedAction("issue_refund", {"id": order_id}, ActionImpact.IRREVERSIBLE, "Issue refund"),
        PlannedAction("send_email", {"id": order_id}, ActionImpact.IRREVERSIBLE, "Email customer"),
    ]
    for action in plan:
        if not approver.confirm(action):
            return f"Workflow stopped at: {action.description}"
        executor.execute(action)
    return "Workflow completed with approvals"
Agency Budget: A Practical Framework
An
agency budget

is a concise specification of the maximum scope an agent is permitted to operate in, reviewed and signed off before deployment. It forces explicit decisions about each dimension of agency before the agent goes live.
Budget item
Specification example
Reviewer check
Tool set
search_catalogue, get_order_status only
Are all listed tools necessary for the task?
DB permissions
SELECT on public.orders WHERE customer_id = session_user
No DDL, no other tables, row-level security in place?
File system scope
READ /data/reports/, WRITE /output/reports/
No access outside these paths; no shell exec?
Network destinations
api.internal.company.com only
Explicit allow-list; no wildcard domains?
Irreversible actions
send_refund_request (human approval required)
Is the approval gate implemented and tested?
Session caps
50 tool calls / session, 5 high-risk / session
Does the dispatcher enforce these limits?
Summary
Excessive agency is a design failure in three dimensions: granting more
permissions
than the task requires, providing more
tools
than the task requires, and allowing the agent to act
autonomously

on irreversible operations without human checkpoints. It is the single most consequential amplifier of all other agentic vulnerabilities — an injection attack in a minimally-privileged agent causes limited harm, while the same attack in an over-provisioned agent can compromise entire systems. The mitigations are structural: dedicate one service account per agent with explicitly scoped IAM and database grants, define task-specific tool sets from a central registry, and require human confirmation before any action that cannot be undone. Audit existing deployments using the three-dimension framework — unused tools, over-permissioned grants, and unguarded irreversible actions — to quantify the current blast radius and prioritise remediation.
